- Added descriptive text to Key Encryption algorithm sections
- Expanded Security Considerations with ML-KEM-512 omission rationale

pulls.title: post-alignment updates
pulls.author: panva
pulls.body:
- **Removed ChaCha20Poly1305 AEAD ciphersuites.** Remaining registrations all use AES-256-GCM.
- **Intentional gaps in Algorithm Name numbering** so a given identifier always denotes the same HPKE KEM/KDF/AEAD combination across JOSE and COSE. Added a note in the IANA Considerations explaining the gaps.
- **New "Security Strength" subsection** in Security Considerations covering NIST PQ security levels for ML-KEM-768/1024, classical floor provided by P-256/X25519/P-384 in the PQ/T hybrids, and rationale for SHAKE256 + AES-256-GCM (with reference to CNSA 2.0). Also explains why AES-256-GCM was selected as the sole AEAD (minimize ciphersuite count, hardware acceleration, broad deployment).
- **Additional security considerations** motivating PQ/T hybrid confidentiality (HNDL) with reference to RFC 9794 Section 5 and `I-D.ietf-pquip-pqc-engineers` Section 13.1, and requiring that in a General JWE JSON Serialization with multiple recipients, all recipients MUST use a quantum-resistant Key Management algorithm.
- **Rationale for registering both families.** Added text explaining why PQ/T hybrid ciphersuites are generally preferred, and why pure PQ ciphersuites are also registered (regulatory/compliance mandates such as CNSA 2.0, and deployments where the overhead of a traditional component is undesirable).
- **RFC 9794 terminology adopted** in Conventions and Definitions for "Traditional Algorithm", "Post-Quantum Algorithm", "PQ/T Hybrid Scheme", and "PQ/T Hybrid KEM"; the local term "pure post-quantum" is defined in the same section.
- **Refactored JSON Web Key Representation section.** Points "AKP" key type to Section 3 of `I-D.ietf-cose-dilithium`; clarifies that `alg` is required and identifies both the HPKE ciphersuite and Integrated vs. Key Encryption usage; specifies `pub`/`priv` as base64url-encoded (no padding, per RFC 4648 Section 5) outputs of `SerializePublicKey()`/`SerializePrivateKey()` from Section 4 of `I-D.ietf-hpke-hpke`.
- **New informative references:** `I-D.ietf-pquip-pqc-engineers`, `RFC9794`, and `CNSA2.0`.
- **Authors:** added Hannes Tschofenig (University of the Bundeswehr Munich) and Tirumaleswar Reddy (Nokia).

comments.author: panva
comments.body:
> Most of the changes look good, I raised few comments.

Thank you, they're good suggestions. I've actioned all of them (to some extent at least). https://github.com/panva/draft-jose-hpke-pq-pqt/pull/3#issuecomment-4266446595

comments.author: panva
comments.body:
@tireddy2 for the automation portion, for COSE do you want a ChaCha variant for all algorithms? (incl. ML-KEM-512 and the rest of the Pure PQ ones?) e.g. 


```
const suites = [
  // PQ/T Hybrid
  [KEM_MLKEM768_P256,   KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_AES_256_GCM,      { jose: true,  cose: true }],
  [KEM_MLKEM768_P256,   KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_ChaCha20Poly1305, { jose: false, cose: true }],
  [KEM_MLKEM768_X25519, KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_AES_256_GCM,      { jose: true,  cose: true }],
  [KEM_MLKEM768_X25519, KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_ChaCha20Poly1305, { jose: false, cose: true }],
  [KEM_MLKEM1024_P384,  KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_AES_256_GCM,      { jose: true,  cose: true }],
  [KEM_MLKEM1024_P384,  KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_ChaCha20Poly1305, { jose: false, cose: true }],
  // Pure PQ
  [KEM_ML_KEM_512,      KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_AES_128_GCM,      { jose: false, cose: true }],
  [KEM_ML_KEM_512,      KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_ChaCha20Poly1305, { jose: false, cose: true }],
  [KEM_ML_KEM_768,      KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_AES_256_GCM,      { jose: true,  cose: true }],
  [KEM_ML_KEM_768,      KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_ChaCha20Poly1305, { jose: false, cose: true }],
  [KEM_ML_KEM_1024,     KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_AES_256_GCM,      { jose: true,  cose: true }],
  [KEM_ML_KEM_1024,     KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_ChaCha20Poly1305, { jose: false, cose: true }],
];
```

Or, as-is, without a ChaCha variant for ML-KEM-512, lacking any further guidance I'm going to assume this one:

```
const suites = [
  // PQ/T Hybrid
  [KEM_MLKEM768_P256,   KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_AES_256_GCM,      { jose: true,  cose: true }],
  [KEM_MLKEM768_P256,   KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_ChaCha20Poly1305, { jose: false, cose: true }],
  [KEM_MLKEM768_X25519, KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_AES_256_GCM,      { jose: true,  cose: true }],
  [KEM_MLKEM768_X25519, KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_ChaCha20Poly1305, { jose: false, cose: true }],
  [KEM_MLKEM1024_P384,  KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_AES_256_GCM,      { jose: true,  cose: true }],
  [KEM_MLKEM1024_P384,  KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_ChaCha20Poly1305, { jose: false, cose: true }],
  // Pure PQ
  [KEM_ML_KEM_512,      KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_AES_128_GCM,      { jose: false, cose: true }],
  [KEM_ML_KEM_768,      KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_AES_256_GCM,      { jose: true,  cose: true }],
  [KEM_ML_KEM_768,      KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_ChaCha20Poly1305, { jose: false, cose: true }],
  [KEM_ML_KEM_1024,     KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_AES_256_GCM,      { jose: true,  cose: true }],
  [KEM_ML_KEM_1024,     KDF_SHAKE256, AEAD_ChaCha20Poly1305, { jose: false, cose: true }],
];
```

comments.author: panva
comments.body: @tireddy2 as [this](https://github.com/panva/draft-jose-hpke-pq-pqt/pull/3#issuecomment-4280099283) informs the gaps left in the JOSE algorithm series we need to know before proceeding with an updated proposal (in the interest of keeping churn of future revisions to the minimum)

No, let's not add the ChaCha variant. Hannes will check if constrained devices are planning to deploy ChaCha20-Poly1305 or not. Please modify it to "false" for now.

comments.author: panva
panva
comments.body: In that case, I believe [this is ready to go](https://github.com/panva/draft-jose-hpke-pq-pqt/pull/3#issuecomment-4266446595) cc @hannestschofenig @bc-pi

comments.author: hannestschofenig
comments.body: Looks good. Really only minor comments

I suggest adding text to justify why both pure PQC and PQ/T hybrid are registered ? 

I propose to add the following text:

Deployments can choose between two strategies: PQ/T hybrid key exchange, which combines traditional and post-quantum algorithms for defense in depth, or pure PQC key exchange, which uses post-quantum algorithms exclusively. Hybrid key exchange is generally preferred because it ensures continued security even if one of the component algorithms is compromised during the transitional period. However, pure PQC key exchange may be required for specific deployments with regulatory or compliance mandates that necessitate the exclusive use of PQC, such as those governed by the Commercial National Security Algorithm Suite 2.0 (CNSA 2.0) {{CNSA2.0}}.

I suggest to add following text: 
This document uses the terms "Traditional Algorithm", "Post-Quantum Algorithm", "PQ/T Hybrid Scheme", and "PQ/T Hybrid KEM" as defined in {{RFC9794}}. The term "pure post-quantum" is used in this document to refer to a single-algorithm scheme using only a post-quantum algorithm, with no traditional component.
ccfda34289bb3c823e1b0b213d0912060fa4a24d

I propose to re-use https://github.com/tireddy2/Hybrid-KEM-with-COSE-JOSE/blob/main/draft-reddy-cose-jose-pqc-hybrid-hpke.md#security-strength-analysis-of-registered-hpke-ciphersuites which has a detailed table covering all registered ciphersuites. In the table, ML-KEM-512 and ChaCha20-Poly1305 has to be excluded.

we may want to add a note that:
ChaCha20-Poly1305 ciphersuites are not defined in this document to minimize the number of ciphersuites and reduce implementation complexity. While both AES-256-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305 provide well-understood and robust security, AES-256-GCM is selected as the sole AEAD algorithm due to its widespread hardware acceleration and broad deployment, and because it provides sufficient security strength for all ciphersuites defined in this document.
I intentionally didn't want to use that table, the prose is simpler to grok and covers all the table had to say.

reviews.author: tireddy2
reviews.body: Most of the changes look good, I raised few comments.

comments.body:
I see the following points are missing in the section.

1. ML-KEM-1024 is paired with P-384 rather than P-521. While P-521 offers higher classical security strength, P-384 already provides a strong classical fallback (192-bit security), is widely implemented, and aligns with existing TLS PQ/T hybrid construction.

2. ChaCha20-Poly1305 ciphersuites are not defined in this document to minimize the number of ciphersuites and reduce implementation complexity. While both AES-256-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305 provide well-understood and robust security, AES-256-GCM is selected as the sole AEAD algorithm due to its widespread hardware acceleration and broad deployment, and because it provides sufficient security strength for all ciphersuites defined in this document.

3. The -KE variants share identical cryptographic properties to their integrated encryption counterparts.

1) this is completely irrelevant because we're working within the constraints of HPKE algorithms registered by `I-D.ietf-hpke-pq` and MLKEM1024-P521 is not one of them. It is therefore covered by `The security considerations of [I-D.ietf-jose-hpke-encrypt] and [I-D.ietf-hpke-pq] apply to this document.`
2) I deliberately did not mention "ChaCha20-Poly1305" by name in the security considerations in 2e71771b320037796c1a6fe73730d21bd98eaba2, the draft never defined those suites in a published version from the registry's perspective, so naming it there creates a phantom. The existing changelog entry "Removed ChaCha20Poly1305 AEAD ciphersuites" is the correct place for that historical fact. The security-considerations text instead gives the forward-looking rationale for why AES-256-GCM is the only AEAD.
3) this is verbatim included in [section 5.1](https://panva.github.io/draft-jose-hpke-pq-pqt/pr-preview/pr-3/draft-skokan-jose-hpke-pq-pqt.html#name-security-strength)
I still think the circumstances that led to this are unnecessary and unfortunate.

reviews.author: bc-pi
reviews.body: Thank you @panva for all the work on this.

comments.body: At this point the gap is just *one* algorithm (the ML-KEM-512 one). We've made it so that ChaCha variants (if there are any in COSE afterall) would come at the end of the series.

Trying to stick with the RFC 9794 terminology here: 

```suggestion
Including a recipient that uses a traditional cryptographic algorithm would
```

```suggestion
used internally by ML-KEM. They also use AES‑256‑GCM as the AEAD. As discussed in
```

I have intentionally worded it as such because traditional would mean that it cannot be combined with traditional symmetric key management modes too, which isn't the case, those are not susceptible and can be used in combination with asymmetric pqc key management modes.
